title: Compliance with Policies — Splunk Search Head Knowledge Object Violations

compliance.assurance_rationale: ISO/IEC 27001 A.5.36 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that ISO 27001 A.5.36 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.6.16: Compliance with Policies — Splunk Search Head Knowledge Object Violations.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.6.16' running on | rest /services/saved/searches, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

spl:
| rest /services/saved/searches splunk_server=local count=0
| search disabled=0 is_scheduled=1
| where !match(search,"(?i)index\s*=\s*") OR match(search,"(?i)index\s*=\s*all")
| table title, eai:acl.owner, search
description: Detects saved searches referencing non-approved indexes or missing required index= constraint for enforced search policies.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for ISO 27001 compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
grandmaExplanation: We catch saved searches referencing non-approved data or missing required data= constraint for enforced search policies — so you can show exactly what is being watched and when something went wrong.
implementation: (1) Define approved index list separately; (2) exclude system apps; (3) quarterly SoA evidence for tooling policies.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise (`_audit`, REST).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: | rest /services/saved/searches.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Define approved index list separately; (2) exclude system apps; (3) quarterly SoA evidence for tooling policies.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
| rest /services/saved/searches splunk_server=local count=0
| search disabled=0 is_scheduled=1
| where !match(search,"(?i)index\s*=\s*") OR match(search,"(?i)index\s*=\s*all")
| table title, eai:acl.owner, search
```

Understanding this SPL

**Compliance with Policies — Splunk Search Head Knowledge Object Violations (A.5.36)** — Detects saved searches referencing non-approved indexes or missing required index= constraint for enforced search policies.

Documented **Data sources**: | rest /services/saved/searches. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise (`_audit`, REST). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Calls Splunk `rest` to read configuration or REST-exposed entities.
• Applies an explicit `search` filter to narrow the current result set.
• Filters the current rows with `where !match(search,"(?i)index\s*=\s*") OR match(search,"(?i)index\s*=\s*all")` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.

Output fields: eai:acl.owner, search, title.

Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with the GRC system, audit register, or framework crosswalk. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table, Bar chart
knownFalsePositives: Access reviews lag during HR transitions, manager changes, or quarterly review cycles.
